pages.text: Long term study on compressive and bond strength of coconut shell aggregate concrete K. Gunasekaran a,b,⇑, R. Annadurai a,b, P.S. Kumar b a Department of Civil Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM University, Kattankulathur 603 203, Tamil Nadu, India b Department of Civil, Anna University, Trichirappalli (Panruti Campus), India article info Article history: Received 20 August 2011 Accepted 21 August 2011 Available online 13 October 2011 Keywords: Coconut shell Concrete Curing Compressive and bond strength SEM images abstract Effects of three types of curing on coconut shell aggregate concrete have been studied for long term per- formance. The pore structure of coconut shell has been studied through scanning electron microscope (SEM). The pore structures in coconut shell behave like a reservoir. Intermittent curing produced the highest coconut shell aggregate concrete strength, followed by full water, and then by air-dry curing. Bio- logical decay was not evident as the concrete cubes gained strength even after 365 days. Up to an age of 90 days, the samples under all types of curing conditions showed improved response on the pulse veloc- ity and subsequently an insigniﬁcant drop. The ultimate bond strength of coconut shell aggregate con- crete under all types of curing conditions was much higher compared to the theoretical bond strength as per BS 8110 and IS 456. Bonding between the cement paste and the coconut shell aggregate has been studied by measuring ﬁssure between the coconut shell and the cement paste through SEM analysis. It shows a tendency of narrowing the ﬁssure due to its age, which shows that the bond appears to be better between the coconut shell and the cement paste. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Coconut is grown in more than 93 countries. South East Asia is regarded as the origin of coconut. India is the third largest, having cultivation on an area of about 1.78 million hectares. Annual pro- duction is about 7562 million nuts with an average of 5295 nuts per hectare. The coconut industry in India accounts for over a quar- ter of the world’s total coconut oil output and is set to grow further with the global increase in demand. However, it is also the main contributor to the nation’s pollution problem as a solid waste in the form of shells, which involves an annual production of approx- imately 3.18 million tones. Coconut shell (CS) represents more than 60% of the domestic waste volume ( www.nmce.com/com- moditystudy/coconut_study.jsp). CS, which presents serious dis- posal problems for local environment, is an abundantly available agricultural waste from local coconut industries. In developing countries where abundant agricultural and industrial wastes are discharged, these wastes can be used as potential material or replacement material in the construction industry. This will have the double advantage of reduction in the cost of construction material and also as a means of disposal of wastes. 2. Background The concrete obtained using CS aggregates satisﬁes the mini- mum requirements of lightweight concrete [1]. Concrete using CS aggregate resulted in acceptable strength required for structural lightweight concrete (LWC). LWC developed from CS aggregate can be used for both structural and non-structural applications [2]. CS may offer itself as a coarse aggregate as well as a potential construction material in the ﬁeld of construction industries and this would solve the en

his research that wood based materials, being hard and of organic origin, will not contaminate or leach to produce toxic substances once they are bound in concrete matrix [7]. 3. Thrust of the study Since the moisture retaining and water absorbing capacity of CS are more compared to conventional aggregate and it is recom- mended to treat the CS before use as an aggregate for water absorption [1–6], the effects of different curing conditions on CS aggregate concrete compressive and bond strength are studied. Attention is given to the inﬂuence of the water absorption of CS and also the inﬂuence of the bonding through SEM analysis be- tween CS aggregate and cement paste. 4. Materials used 4.1. CS as aggregate The freshly discarded CS collected from a local oil mill was used in this study. Since the different species of CS are processed together, the shells are found to have varying thicknesses of 2–8 mm. After crushing, the shells are ﬂaky and irregularly shaped. For preparing CS aggregates, a crusher was designed, manufactured, and in- stalled at SRM University. Since CS is a ﬂaky material, the sizes of CS lengthwise are restricted to a maximum of 12 mm ( Fig. 1), to get enough workability with the con- crete. The surface texture of the shell was fairly smooth on concave and rough on convex faces. The crushed edges were rough and spiky. CS aggregates have a rela- tively high water absorption value nearly, 24%, compared to the conventional aggregate (0.5%); hence, to prevent water absorption by the concrete mix, it is nec- essary to mix at Saturated Surface Dry condition (SSD) based on 24 h submersion in potable water. The CS was examined using SEM and analyzed. The SEM images on CS are shown in Fig. 2. Fig. 3 shows SEM images on CS without and with soaking of 24 h duration. 4.2. Other concrete mix constituents Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) 53 Grade conforming to Indian Standard IS 12269: 1987 [8] is used as a binder. River sand (Palar river bed) is used throughout the investigation as ﬁne aggregate conforming to grading zone III as per IS 383-1970 [9]. The potable water available in the University premises was used for mixing and curing. 5. Mix design The mix design for lightweight concrete used for structural purposes depends on the physical and mechanical properties of lightweight aggregate. No speciﬁc methods are available for the design of lightweight concrete mixes. Lightweight concrete mix design is usually established by trial mixes. ACI and Indian Stan- dard methods could not be applied to the mix design of concrete with agro-waste materials [10]. For the production of coconut shell aggregate concrete (CSAC), enough trial mixes through weigh batches were already made and established for optimization of a mix ratio by considering cement content, wood–cement ratio, and water–cement ratio [5]. The selected and established mix ratio for CSAC is 1:1.47:0.65:0.42 by weight of cement (Cement: Sand: CS: Water) in which cement content was set 510 kg/m 3 and this mix also satisﬁes the criteria of structural LWC as per ASTM C 330 [5]. This established mix ratio was taken for this study. 6. Experimental programme Three different types of curing regimes were considered and the effects of these on CSAC compressive strength and bond strength were studied over a 365 days for the selected mix. Ambient tem- peratures ranged from 27 /C176C±7 /C176C with relative humidity in the range of 40–90%. The different curing regimes adopted were full immersi

mal curing practices adopted in situ conditions. In this type of cur- ing, the exposure site was unobstructed from wind, sun radiation and rainfall. No curing (i.e.) full air drying of specimens (W3) which is the practical form of curing for precast concrete. In this curing specimens were left exposed in the laboratory after demolding. 6.1. Test samples for compressive strength of CSAC All samples were cast in cube molds (100 /C2100 /C2100 mm) as per IS: 516-1959 [11]. Workability of fresh concrete and the 28- day air-dry density were measured in accordance with IS: 1199- 1959 [12]. Compressive strengths were measured using a compres- sion testing machine with a maximum capacity of 2000 kN. For all tests, each value was taken as the average of three samples. 6.2. Test samples for bond strength of CSAC The bond strength was determined using the pull-out test and the specimens were prepared as per IS 2770 (Part-I 1967) [13]. The specimens were of 100 mm diameter and 200 mm height incorporating both deformed and plain bars of 8, 10, 12 and 6 mm diameters. The details of specimen preparation, the testing methodology and the computation of bond strength were reported elsewhere [5]. 6.3. Ultrasonic pulse velocity (UPV) The pulse velocity methods have been used to evaluate concrete structures and attempts have been made to correlate the pulse velocity with quality and other properties of concrete. For estab- lishing the uniformity of concrete, the ultrasonic concrete tester is an ideal tool for laboratory specimens, hence in all the three dif- ferent curing types, UPV tests were also conducted and analyzed. The concrete qualities are analyzed with the help of velocity crite- rion for concrete as recommended by IS: 13311-Part I [14–15] which are applicable for the normal conventional aggregate con- crete and this has been taken as reference. 6.4. Bonding study through SEM In any composite material the physical and chemical properties of the constituents and the interactions between them determine the behavior of the material. Concrete is also a composite material with coarse and ﬁne aggregate embedded in a cement paste ma- trix. Thus the mechanical behavior and the durability of the con- crete are affected by the aggregate and the cement paste as well as the bonding between them. A weak bond will have a serious ef- fect on strength and durability. It will provide ready access for moisture, water and chemical attack into the concrete resulting in deterioration of both concrete and reinforcement. For concrete to perform satisfactorily it is essential that the bond between the cement paste and aggregate should be as dense as possible. Hence, the micro structure ﬁssure between CS and the cement paste was examined by SEM analyzes. 7. Results and discussions 7.1. SEM analysis on CS Fig. 2a and b shows the SEM images of CS specimens. CS speci- mens have very closely spaced discrete cells having sizes between 16.36 lm and 29.33 lm and within the cells, it has micro-pores with sizes varying from 760.6 nm to 1.64 lm. Similarly, CS speci- mens also have some long continuous chain linked cells with dif- ferent widths varying 7.35–8.88 lm. The thickness of continuous cells in the direction of widths varies in the range of 852.7 nm– 1.24 lm. These discrete and continuous chain linked cells of CS specimens could be the reason for resisting more impact, crushing and abrasion resistance of CS specimens. Fig. 3 a and b shows the SEM images of CS specimens withou

polystyrene and crushed lightweight bricks [16–18]. Compressive strength of LWC depends on both strength of the matrix and parti- cle strength of the aggregate. Also, compressive strength of LWC is usually related to the cement content at a given slump and air con- tent, rather than to w/c ratio. This is due to the difﬁculty in deter- mining how much of the total mix water is absorbed by the aggregate and thus not available for reaction with the cement [19]. However, in this study CS aggregates were used in SSD condi- tion and the calculated free water for the mix to obtain desired workability. 7.3. Effects of curing conditions on compressive strength The developments of the compressive strengths of the CSAC cube samples under three curing regimes are shown in Fig. 4 .I t was found that intermittent curing conditions produced the high- est CSAC strength, followed by full water curing, and then air-dry curing. In case of W1 curing, water is available throughout its cur- ing period and this creates lubrication between CS and the cement paste. It may also have reduced the heat of hydration. In the case of W2 curing, water is not available throughout its curing period and at the time of lack of water, the absorbed water from the CS pore structure reservoir would help for continuous hydration process and hence there is no chance of developing lubrication between the CS and the cement paste. Since there is no water supply in the case of W3 curing, the water absorbed by CS during the time of soaking would help the hydration process in the CSAC to some extent. It was observed that most of the compressive strength development takes place in the early stages and the compressive strength continues to increase with age. Biological decay was not evident as the cubes gained strength even after 365 days. This con- tinual increase in strength indicates that the CSAC does not deteri- orate once CS aggregates are encapsulated into the concrete matrix. Similar trends were reported for OPS in the literature [20]. 7.4. Ultrasonic pulse velocity (UPV) Fig. 5 presents the variation of UPV with curing age of CSAC un- der three types of curing conditions. At 28 days, UPV values were 4510, 4570 and 4170 m/s in all types of curing conditions and hence the CSAC can be graded as good. Samples under all types of curing showed CSAC to be of poor quality in early ages (before 28-days). Throughout the period, the samples under W2 produced highest pulse velocities for all ages of tests followed by W1, and W3. The same trends were observed in the case of compressive strength as well. Up to an age of 90-days, the samples under all types of curing conditions showed improved response on the pulse velocity and subsequently an insigniﬁcant drop. Since, there are no large differences in pulse velocities are found in three different cur- ing conditions on CSAC; it show that there is an uniformity and no defects are in CSAC. 7.5. Effects of curing conditions on bond strength The theoretical bond strengths for plain bars in conventional concrete are 1.4 and 1.45 N/mm 2 as per IS 456-2000 and BS 8110, respectively. The corresponding values for deformed bars are 2.24 and 2.58 N/mm 2 [5]. From the tests carried out, it was ob- served that most of the bond strength development takes place in the early stages. For all specimens there were signiﬁcant incre- ments in the bond strength up to the age of 56 days under all three types curing conditions. After 56 days, the bond strength deve

strength). In W2 curing, the bond strength of specimens with plain bars ranged from 3.77 to 7.96 N/mm 2 (14–29% of compressive strength). For deformed bars, the bond strength ranged from 4.55 to 10.41 N/mm 2 (17–38% of compressive strength). In W3 curing, the bond strength of specimens with plain bars ranged from 3.59 to 5.54 N/mm2 (14–21% of compressive strength) and for deformed bars, the bond strength ranged from 4.02 to 8.24 N/mm 2 (15–32% of compressive strength). Similar trends were reported by Teo et al. [21]. Figs. 6–11 illustrate the bond strength development un- der different curing conditions. Under intermittent curing condition, the highest bond strength was achieved, followed by full water curing, and then by air-dried curing. It is interesting to note that the bond strength was rela- tively higher for specimens with W2 curing than for W1 condition. The reason is the same as explained for compressive strength above. It was found that, there was no signiﬁcant difference in bond strength development in W1 and W2 curing. In general, the bond strength of CSAC is comparable to the bond strength of nor- mal and other LWC [21]. In all types of curing at all ages of the testing, plain bars failed by pulling out of the concrete without any visible cracks. When the plain bar is in tension, the adhesion and friction forces are lost as the diameter of the bar decreases slightly causing the bars to slip, which ﬁnally leads to failure. Deformed bars failed by concrete cover cracking and the failures were sudden with the formation of longitudinal cracks. It was observed that cracks progressed over the entire length of the specimen before failure occurred. In case of plain bars, the absence of anchorages and smooth surface on one side of the CS aggregates coupled with the continuous pres- ence of water might have prevented good bond between the smooth bars, which contributed to the lower bond strength. How- ever, even this lower bond strength value for plain bars is greater than the theoretical prediction as per standards BS 8110 and IS 456 [5]. In general, the ultimate bond strength of CS aggregate con- crete under all types of curing conditions was much higher than the theoretical bond strength. As expected, the bond strength of specimens with deformed bars was much higher than that of plain bars. In this study, the bond strength of deformed bars was approximately higher by 18–24%, 21–31%, 12–48% compared to specimens with plain bars under W1, W2, and W3 curing conditions respectively. It was ob- served that for both plain and deformed bars, as the bar size in- creases, the bond strength decreases. This is because of the surrounding volume of concrete and hence the conﬁning pressure reduces on the reinforcing bar as the sizes increase. Consequently, the conﬁning pressure on the reinforcing bar from the surrounding concrete is also decreased, resulting in the decrease of bond strength in this speciﬁc size of specimen. Similar trends were re- Fig. 6. Bond strength development of CSAC of plain bars (W1-curing). Fig. 7. Bond strength development of CSAC of plain bars (W2 curing). 212 K. Gunasekaran et al. / Construction and Building Materials 28 (2012) 208–215

ported by Kankam [22], Cusens and Yu [23], and Short and Kinni- burgh [24]. 7.6. Bonding through SEM Study on bond through SEM was done on the CSAC mix in full water curing condition only. A microstructure of the ﬁssure between the cement paste and CS at 3 days, 7 days and 28 days are shown in Fig. 12 a–c respectively. The ﬁssure zone (black area) and the cement paste can be easily distinguished. A part to the left of black area in the ﬁgures is CS aggregate and a part to the right of black area is cement paste. It is noted that the non-continuous region between the CS aggregate and the cement paste which is approximately 52.31–88.27 lm thick, 41.72–47.96 lm thick, and 24.94–26.63 lm thick at 3 days, 7 days, and 28 days respectively. From these results, it is clear that proper rate of hydration and the hydration products were in the progressive stage and there was development in the rate of gain in compressive strength. The ﬁssure zone between the CS and the cement paste shows a ten- dency of narrowing the ﬁssures due to age of concrete. This shows that the bond appears to be better and exist proper interlocking be- tween the coconut shell and the cement paste. Fig. 13 shows the cross sectional view of CSAC. Fig. 13a was ta- ken at 28 days and Fig. 13 b was taken after 2 years on the same Fig. 12. Fissures between cement paste and coconut shell aggregate (a), (b), and (c). Fig. 11. Bond strength development of CSAC of deformed bars (W3 curing). Fig. 13. Cross-sectional view of CSAC (a) at 28 days (b) at 2 years. 214 K. Gunasekaran et al. / Construction and Building Materials 28 (2012) 208–215

specimen. There is no visible change on CS and no deterioration of exposed surface of concrete specimen. Sample specimen were tested at 2 years and found that there was no loss in the CSAC com- pressive strength. Hence, it is clear that CS aggregate is not de- graded due to its age in the concrete. The interfacial zone between the CS aggregate and the cement paste was investigated using hand held microscope with sensitivity 0.01 mm on a cross section. It was found that there was no formation of cracks and voids between the CS aggregate and the cement paste. Hence it is concluded that there is no bond failure between CS and the ce- ment paste even at the later ages. 8. Conclusions There is need to treat the coconut shell before use as an aggre- gate for water absorption. Coconut shell specimens has very closely spaced discrete cells and also has some long continuous chain linked cells with different widths. The water absorbed by the coco- nut shell during the course of soaking is stored and the pore struc- tures in the coconut shell behave like a reservoir. Intermittent curing condition produced the highest coconut shell aggregate con- crete strength, followed by full water curing, and then by air-dries curing. Most of the compressive strength development takes place in the early stages and continues to increase with age indicates that the coconut shell aggregate concrete does not deteriorate once coconut shell aggregates are encapsulated into the concrete matrix. Biological decay was not evident as the cubes gained strength even after 365 days. Cube samples under all types of curing showed coconut shell aggregate concrete in a good quality in later ages. The ultimate bond strength of coconut shell aggregate concrete un- der all types of curing conditions was much higher compared to the theoretical bond strength as per BS 8110 and IS 456. In coconut shell aggregate concrete no bond failure even at the later ages and maintained good quality for the long-term performance. Acknowledgements The authors wish to thank the SRM University Management, for their support to complete this study and those who were directly or indirectly involved in this study. Also, we express our thanks to Mr. C. Gopalakrishnan, Center head, and Mr. K.R. Ganesh, re- search scholar, Nano Technology Research Center, SRM University for their help in SEM analysis.
